Title: Hamas to meet Egyptian mediators over alleged Gaza truce violations by Israel
Article:
GAZA CITY: A delegation from Hamas is set to hold talks with Egyptian mediators in Cairo on Sunday to address alleged Israeli violations of the Gaza ceasefire, according to two officials from the Palestinian group.
Despite the ceasefire that took effect in October, violence has continued to unsettle the Palestinian territory.
Both Israel and Hamas have accused each other of breaching the US-backed truce, which has largely halted the two-year war that devastated the enclave of 2.2 million.
"The delegation will meet Egyptian officials on Sunday morning to discuss halting Israeli violations and implementing the remaining provisions of the first phase of the agreement," a Hamas official told AFP, speaking on condition of anonymity as he was not authorised to speak publicly.
According to the official, Hamas will emphasise the need for Israel to cease all violations of the truce, dismantle its military positions in Gaza, fully reopen border crossings, increase the flow of travellers, and allow greater volumes of humanitarian aid into the territory.
The group is also expected to press for a US-established Gaza administrative body of Palestinian technocrats to enter the enclave and assume its governing responsibilities.
A second Hamas official added that the delegation would also meet representatives of other Palestinian factions in Cairo to discuss these issues.
In January, Washington announced that the ceasefire had moved into its second phase under a peace plan brokered by President Donald Trump.
This phase envisages the disarmament of Hamas and a gradual withdrawal of Israeli forces from Gaza.
It also calls for the establishment of a 20,000-strong peacekeeping force, called the international stabilisation force, to which several countries have committed troops.
Hamas says it is not opposed to handing over part of its arsenal, but only as part of a Palestinian political process.
Meanwhile, violence persists in Gaza.
On Saturday, the territory's civil defence agency, which operates as a rescue force under Hamas authority, reported that Israeli airstrikes killed seven people in a northern district.
The Israeli military said it had struck an "armed terrorist cell". It said fighters had approached what is known as the yellow line, the de facto boundary dividing Gaza into two zones: one under Israeli military control and one under Hamas control.
At least 749 Palestinians have been killed since the truce began, according to Gaza's health ministry, which is also under Hamas authority and whose figures are considered reliable by the United Nations.
The Israeli army has reported five soldiers killed in Gaza since the start of the truce.
Media restrictions and limited access in Gaza have prevented AFP from independently verifying casualty figures or freely covering the fighting.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Jail time for watch gallery employee over RM2.6 million timepiece theft
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A 24-year-old employee of a luxury watch gallery has been sentenced to a year's imprisonment and one stroke of the rotan after admitting to stealing 12 high-end timepieces worth RM2.6 million from his workplace in Bukit Jalil.
Cheras police chief Assistant Commissioner Mohd Rosdi Daud said the theft took place about 12.10pm on March 31 at the watch gallery, when the suspect took advantage of his colleague's absence from the front counter.
"He was captured on closed-circuit television (CCTV) camera removing 12 luxury watches from the display counter and placing them into a black backpack before leaving the premises," he said in a statement today.
Rosdi said the suspect was arrested the following day, on April 1, by a team from the Kuala Lumpur police headquarters Criminal Investigation Department at the parking area of a mall at Bukit Damansara.
"Police also seized a Rolex Oyster Perpetual watch, an iPhone 16 Pro Max and four pawnshop receipts amounting to RM672,000
"The case was initially investigated under Section 381 of the Penal Code for theft by an employee, before the investigation papers were referred to the deputy public prosecutor on April 2," he said.
He added that further instructions were to charge the suspect under Section 408 of the Penal Code for criminal breach of trust by an employee.
"The accused was charged at the Jalan Duta court on April 3, where he pleaded guilty.
"He was sentenced to a year's imprisonment from the date of conviction, one stroke of the rotan, and fined RM20,000, in default an additional 12 months' jail," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran's ex-FM Zarif blames talks failure on US push to 'dictate' terms
Article:
TEHERAN: Iran's former foreign minister Mohammad Javad Zarif, who led his country's delegation at 2015 nuclear talks, on Sunday blamed the failure of negotiations to end the Middle East war on US attempts to "dictate" its terms.
"No negotiations - at least with Iran - will succeed based on 'our/your terms'," said Zarif, one of the architects of the nuclear deal with the United States and other world powers, which was abandoned in 2018 by US President Donald Trump.
"The US must learn: you can't dictate terms to Iran. It's not too late to learn. Yet," added Zarif in a post on X.
On Saturday, senior American and Iranian officials held peace talks in Pakistan, as a fragile two-week ceasefire held.
After the talks failed, US news website Axios quoted an unnamed source briefed on the negotiations as saying that disagreements included "Iran's demand to control the Strait of Hormuz and refusal to give up on its enriched uranium stockpile."
US Vice President JD Vance, who led his country's delegation, said at a press conference Sunday that the talks lasted around 21 hours and that Iran had "chosen not to accept our terms."
Iran's foreign ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baqaei said the two sides had agreed on some points but that "differences remained on two or three important issues."
Iran has been at war with the United States and Israel since February 28, when strikes killed the Islamic Republic's supreme leader Ali Khamenei, triggering a conflict that quickly spread across the Middle East.
It was not immediately clear whether the two sides would resume contacts, or what would happen to the ceasefire after talks failed.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ketengah key driver of sustainable region development - Zahid
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Central Terengganu Development Authority (Ketengah) has strong potential to continue advancing as a key drier of sustainable regional development, said Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi.
He said that since its incorporation in 1973, Ketengah has played a significant role in improving the socio-economic development of rural communities in Terengganu.
"Many changes have been undertaken and the outcomes remain evident to the people to this day," he said in a Facebook post in conjunction with the agency's 53rd anniversary.
Zahid, who is also rural and regional development minister, said Ketengah also has strong potential to further strengthen the eco-tourism sector, supported by its natural attractions and unique local heritage.
He said that with structured planning and consistent implementation, Ketengah can help create more economic opportunities for the local community.
"I pray that Ketengah will continue to achieve greater success in the future for the well-being of the people and regional development," he said.
Incorporated on April 12, 1973, Ketengah is an agency under the Rural and Regional Development Ministry tasked with implementing economic and social development within a 653,075ha area covering the interior regions of Dungun, Kemaman and Hulu Terengganu.
–Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysian homeopathy facing  generational gap
Article:
LETTERS:
Homeopathy in Malaysia is entering a more formal phase with the enforcement of the Traditional and Complementary Medicine Act 2016 within the framework of mandatory registration for recognised practice areas.
It signals that homeopathy has been given a place within the country's traditional and complementary healthcare landscape.
Another positive development is the government's decision that traditional and complementary medicine services are not subject to service tax.
This suggests that, at the policy level, there is some understanding that the field should be given room to grow rather than being further burdened.
The larger question now is how this field can continue to grow ethically, on a sound knowledge base, with sustainable education and career pathways, and in ways that genuinely benefit the Malaysian public.
In recent years, awareness of the need for registration has increased, but the gap between awareness and  registration remains significant.
Practitioners are facing costs, continuing training requirements, and uncertainty about their future career prospects.
A major issue that is often not fully understood by the public is the generational divide within Malaysian homeopathy.
It is not merely about age. It also reflects differences in training pathways, clinical experience, professional standing and expectations for the future of the field.
The first group is the pioneer generation, generally those in their 60s to 80s. They built homeopathic practices at a time when the field had yet to receive formal recognition.
The second group is the middle generation, usually those in their 40s to 50s. This is often the group in the most difficult position.
Many in this group possess extensive clinical experience, work full-time and form the backbone of many homeopathic centres.
Yet in formal discussions on the future of the profession, they are sometimes not given the recognition they deserve.
The third group is the younger generation, mostly in their 20s to 30s. They come through formal education, meet current academic standards and should ideally become the main  successors of Malaysian homeopathy.
They face a different set of challenges: long training periods, high educational costs and career opportunities that remain insufficiently clear.
When the age gap between these groups can stretch from 20 to 40 years, the challenge is clearly not a small one. It is about how they can build professional credibility and envision the future of homeopathy itself.
If this divide is not bridged wisely, the profession will continue to move in fragmented directions even though all are operating within the same field.
The public also needs to understand the organisational structure of homeopathy in Malaysia.
The Malaysian Homeopathic Medical Council (MPHM) is the main body responsible for coordinating the direction of the  profession in Malaysia.
Malaysian Bumiputera Homeopathic Medical Association is one of the member associations within MPHM.
As the structure of member associations has changed over time, the role of the remaining associations has become increasingly important in supporting continuity, representation and professional development within the field.
Ultimately, the future of Malaysian homeopathy will not be determined by legislation alone. It will be determined by how far all parties are willing to work together to build a fair, inclusive, and resilient ecosystem.
Maryam KJ, Rhom(Mal)
Secretary-General
Malaysian Bumiputera Homeopathic Medical Association
The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hassan Karim: PKR faces defining test as internal rivalries threaten party unity
Article:
PASIR GUDANG: Parti Keadilan Rakyat (PKR) is facing one of its most critical tests since assuming federal power, as internal rivalries and external political pressure threaten its unity and survival, Hassan Abdul Karim said today.
The Pasir Gudang member of parliament warned that the party, which anchors the unity government led by Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim, must urgently manage intensifying leadership competition within its ranks to avoid a damaging split.
He cautioned that failure to do so could weaken the party at a time when opposition forces are actively seeking to topple the government.
"PKR, as a governing party, is now facing a major test, both from external political opponents and internal leadership contests that, if not handled wisely, could lead to division," he said in his speech at the PKR Pasir Gudang annual general meeting here today.
Hassan said that differences in views and political positions among party members were inevitable, but warned that such divisions must not come at the expense of the party's stability.
"The strength, unity and survival of the party must take precedence. PKR must not be weakened or defeated due to internal disputes," he said.
He added that criticism of the leadership, including Anwar, was necessary for reform and improvement, but should not escalate to efforts that could undermine the party's legitimacy or leadership structure.
"Criticism is acceptable and necessary, but it must not reach a level that causes greater harm, such as splitting the party or toppling its leadership," he said.
Hassan also rejected the notion that PKR is centred around any single individual, stressing that it remains a party of the people with more than one million members nationwide.
"PKR does not belong to Anwar Ibrahim or his family. It belongs to the rakyat," he said, while acknowledging Anwar's pivotal role in transforming the party from an opposition movement into the backbone of the current administration.
The remarks come amid heightened political manoeuvring ahead of upcoming elections, with PKR expected to defend its position within the multi-coalition government.
Hassan warned that loyalty and resilience would be crucial as the party navigates what he described as a challenging political landscape.
"The struggle is far from over. The road ahead will continue to be filled with challenges, and loyalty to the cause will be tested," he said.
PKR, formed out of the Reformasi movement in the late 1990s, now stands at a crossroads, balancing its reformist roots with the demands and pressures of governing as it braces for the next electoral cycle.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: DBKL: Op Lens to keep Kuala Lumpur orderly, not to cut off earnings
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The activities of unlicensed street photographers touting their services can cause public discomfort and pose safety risks to the community, according to Kuala Lumpur City Hall (DBKL).
DBKL said enforcement actions in the past, including the implementation of 'Op Lens', were not intended to stifle anyone's source of income.
Instead, DBKL said the measures were taken to ensure that the federal capital remains orderly, safe, and free from situations that could adversely affect the experience of city dwellers and tourists.
"While some intend to capture beautiful moments, the reality is that these unregulated activities can lead to unease, public nuisance, and even unnecessary safety risks within our urban spaces.
"Therefore, every location, especially high-traffic tourist areas such as the vicinity of KLCC and the city centre, must remain safe, comfortable, and pleasant for everyone," the local authority said in a post on its Facebook page today.
It said Kuala Lumpur's image as a friendly, harmonious, and liveable city can only be achieved when all parties play their part, respect regulations, maintain proper conduct in public spaces, and collectively preserve the city's reputation.
"Kuala Lumpur is not just a capital city. It is the face of the nation that reflects the experiences and memories of every visitor who comes here. Kuala Lumpur belongs to all of us," it said.
Media outlets previously reported that unauthorised street photographers had been warned to cease their activities in the federal capital, particularly around tourist hotspots, following various issues involving them.
In a recent operation, DBKL issued compounds to six people, including five foreigners, and seized equipment in a crackdown on
unauthorised photography
at the KLCC area.
On April 6, the government decided not to issue
special permits or licences
to individuals touting unofficial photography services at several popular tourist locations in the capital.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran aims to restore majority of refining capability within two months
Article:
DUBAI: Iran expects to restore most damaged refining and distribution facilities to 70–80 per cent of their pre-attack capacity within one to two months, a senior oil official said, as authorities work to recover from a wave of strikes on energy infrastructure.
Deputy Minister of Oil Mohammad Sadeq Azimifar told the Student News Network that repair work had begun and that part of the Lavan refinery is expected to resume operations within about 10 days, with other units coming back online gradually.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Saudi says east-west pipeline, energy facilities restored after attacks
Article:
RIYADH: Saudi Arabia's energy ministry said on Sunday that its key east-west oil pipeline and other facilities had been restored following attacks by Iran on targets across the Gulf.
"Energy facilities and the east-west pipeline damaged by attacks have recovered and regained their operational capacity, enhancing the reliability of supplies," the official Saudi Press Agency reported, citing an energy ministry statement.
Before Tuesday's start of a fragile, two-week truce between Iran and the United States, the Islamic republic had targeted critical energy infrastructure belonging to its oil-rich neighbours.
Tehran accused the Gulf states of allowing US forces to carry out attacks from their territories during the war, which was triggered by Israeli-US strikes on Iran on Feb 28.
The Gulf states have vehemently denied the Iranian accusations.
Saudi Arabia said operational and technical work had led to "restoring full pumping capacity through the east-west pipeline, amounting to approximately seven million barrels per day".
The energy ministry explained the prior attacks had led to the "loss of approximately 700,000 barrels per day of pumping capacity through the east-west pipeline".
Iran's campaign had impacted output at Saudi's Manifa field, where production was now restored, the ministry said, and at the Khurais oil field, where work was underway to restore full production capacity.
On Friday, Riyadh confirmed the weeks-long Iranian attacks had impacted oil production capacity and killed one person.
Three people in total have been killed in Saudi Arabia since the start of the war.
An energy ministry official told the SPA news agency that one of the pumping stations on a vital east-west pipeline was hit in the attacks.
Saudi Arabia's Petroline proved an economic lifeline during the war, with the 750-mile network of pipes connecting the Red Sea to the west with the Gulf in the east as Iran effectively closed the waterway's critical Strait of Hormuz chokepoint.
Facilities in Riyadh, the Eastern Province and the industrial city of Yanbu as well as infrastructure for oil and gas production, transport and refining, and petrochemical plants and power facilities were targeted in the Iranian attacks on Saudi Arabia, the official said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Artemis II lunar mission draws flood of conspiracy theories
Article:
FROM false claims that a historic lunar fly-by was staged in a movie studio to unfounded narratives that footage of the crew was AI-generated, the Artemis II mission has been clouded by a blizzard of misinformation.
The falsehoods – circulating across tech platforms including X, TikTok and Facebook – have also added fresh fuel to a longstanding conspiracy theory that NASA's 1969 Apollo 11 moon landing was faked.
Hashtags such as "fake space" and "fake NASA" have gained traction online since NASA's lunar fly-by sent astronauts farther from Earth than any human before.
Among the falsehoods was an image, viewed over a million times on X, purporting to show the Artemis II crew floating before a green screen and facing film cameras – suggesting their mission was staged in a studio, but in reality bore the hallmarks of AI manipulation.
Some users also shared a video showing text appearing through the mission's official mascot as purported proof the flight was staged.
But a digital forensics expert told AFP's fact-checkers that the anomaly was the result of a failed text overlay by a news station that had syndicated the official feed.
Unfounded claims that the Artemis II mission detected a mysterious moving object on the moon's surface also racked up millions of views across platforms.
The misinformation spread as four astronauts – preparing on Friday for a high-stakes re-entry and splashdown – captivated the world with stunning visuals from their fly-by of the Earth's natural satellite from aboard the Orion spacecraft.
Once confined to the internet's fringes, conspiracy theories have moved squarely into the mainstream amid growing mistrust of public institutions and traditional media.
Scientific achievements such as the lunar mission present "very easy content for conspiracy influencers," said disinformation researcher Mike Rothschild.
"There are some people whose reflexive reaction to any kind of major event is to claim it's fake and staged, no matter what it is," Rothschild told AFP.
Many of them "pass themselves off as experts in science and physics because it's somehow more believable to their followers than just going with 'the official story.'"
The trend underscores a Wild West internet landscape that is largely bereft of guardrails as false narratives erode digital trust.
Several tech platforms have gutted trust and safety teams and scaled back moderation, making them what researchers call a hotbed for misinformation.
[[nid:1416061]]
Further sowing online confusion were claims that the entire Artemis II mission was a hoax powered by artificial intelligence tools.
The assertion underscores how the rise of cheap and widely available AI tools has given misinformation peddlers a handy incentive to cast doubt on authentic content – a tactic researchers have dubbed as the "liar's dividend."
The swirl of falsehoods has also bolstered one of the longest enduring conspiracy theories – that NASA faked the 1969 Apollo 11 moon landing, broadcasting visuals shot in a Hollywood studio.
The conspiratorial discourse has seeped into pop culture, becoming a plotline in movies like romantic comedy "Fly Me to the Moon" – with Scarlett Johansson's character tasked with faking a moon landing – and some celebrities also amplifying the claim.
"The moon landing is an example of a conspiracy that will not die," Timothy Caulfield, a misinformation expert from the University of Alberta in Canada, told AFP.
"These conspiracies are attractive for a host of reasons including that they are linked to the allure of having 'secret knowledge' or being aware of things others don't know."
Though easy to debunk, such theories persist as Artemis II comes decades after the previous lunar missions, events today's internet-savvy generation has little recollection of.
"In many ways, it is a testament to how hard it is for humans to travel to the moon – after all, we did it from 1968 to 1972, and it has taken until 2026 to do it again. It makes many people wonder if it ever happened," space exploration expert Francis French told AFP.
"Right now we are seeing remarkable photographs and video of the Earth and the moon...These photos alone should remove doubt and show once again the amazing things humans are capable of."
* The writers are from AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ukraine, Russia trade Easter truce breach barbs
Article:
KYIV: Ukraine and Russia accused each other on Sunday of violating a truce in place for Orthodox Easter thousands of times, as the war dragged on into its fifth year.
Both sides had agreed to observe the ceasefire for the religious holiday, with Russian President Vladimir Putin on Thursday ordering the truce more than a week after Ukrainian counterpart Volodymyr Zelenskyy proposed a halt to hostilities.
"As of 7am on 12 April, 2,299 ceasefire violations were recorded. Specifically: 28 enemy assault actions, 479 enemy shellings, 747 strikes by attack drones... and 1,045 strikes by FPV drones," the Ukrainian military's general staff said in a post on Facebook.
"There were no missile strikes, guided aerial bomb strikes, or Shahed-type UAV strikes," it added.
In turn, Russia's defence ministry accused Kyiv of nearly 2,000 breaches of its own.
"A total of 1,971 ceasefire violations by units of the Ukrainian armed forces were recorded between 4pm Moscow time on April 12 and 8:00 am on April 12," the ministry said, as reported by the TASS news agency.
The Russian ministry claimed Kyiv had fired 258 times using artillery or tanks, carried out 1,329 FPV drone strikes, and dropped "various types of munitions" on 375 occasions, notably via drones.
Moscow also accused the Ukrainian military of launching "three nighttime attacks" against Russian positions and also "four attempts to advance" along the front line, while claiming to have thwarted each.
The truce had been due to last for 32 hours, from 4pm (1300 GMT) on Saturday until the end of the day on Sunday, according to the Kremlin.
A similar ceasefire was announced in Ukraine for Orthodox Easter last year, only for both sides to accuse each other of numerous violations.
Recent months have seen several rounds of US-brokered negotiations fail to bring the warring parties closer to an agreement to stop the fighting, triggered by Russia's Feb 2022 invasion.
The process has stalled further since the outbreak of the war in the Middle East, with Washington's attention having shifted towards Iran.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Segambut petrol station in viral video: Fault due to missing auto cut-off system - Fuziah
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Irregularities at a diesel pump at a petrol station on Jalan Segambut were due to the lack of an automatic cut-off feature when fuel stock was depleted.
Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Minister Datuk Dr Fuziah Salleh said an investigation by the Kuala Lumpur enforcement team found that although the diesel supply had run out during an ongoing refuelling process, the pump meter continued to record the payment amount.
"Investigations found that the station was not equipped with an automatic volume detection system or an automatic cut-off feature when fuel stock was depleted, and instead relied on manual inspection (manual dipping).
"The actual amount of diesel dispensed was RM232.07 and the station took immediate action by refunding the full payment to the customer involved on April 9. The complainant has agreed to the settlement," she said in a statement today.
Earlier, a viral video alleging irregularities at a diesel pump at the petrol station circulated on social media.
Fuziah added that a technical inspection found all fuel pumps at the premises were still within the valid verification period, while tests using a 20-litre liquid volume measurement showed readings within permitted deviation limits.
"In addition, a review of licences under the Control of Supplies Act 1961 found that the station's CSA (Controlled Scheduled Articles) and PDA (Petrol Dispensing Authorisation) licences are valid and still in force.
"However, the station has been given a stern warning to ensure full compliance with all legal requirements and to increase operational monitoring to prevent such incidents from recurring."
Fuziah said the incident was isolated and did not reflect the overall condition of the fuel supply system, which remains stable and under control.
"In this regard, the public is advised not to speculate or spread unverified information.
"The ministry will continue to intensify monitoring and enforcement to ensure consumers' interests are always protected."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fadillah: We can't afford to rely on just one source of energy
Article:
SRI AMAN: The government has highlighted the importance of broadening the energy mix to ensure a sustainable and resilient supply capable of withstanding future global challenges.
Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Fadillah Yusof said the country could not afford to depend on a single energy source such as water or gas alone.
Fadillah, who is also energy transition and water transformation minister, said diversifying energy sources must be prioritised as the world increasingly faces extreme weather events that could destabilise energy supply.
"We need to be prepared in terms of energy — including gas, biofuel, biomass, solar, and wind — so that we have a good energy mix," he said after the Petra Skuad Zon Sarawak programme at the Batang Ai Hydroelectric Dam in Lubok Antu, here today.
Fadillah said the approach was essential to ensure the country always had alternative energy sources should disruptions occur to the global energy supply structure.
He also stressed that energy security must go hand in hand with affordability, ensuring that both the public and industry could bear the cost of supply.
"At the same time, we must also care for the environment in our efforts to develop the nation's energy sector," he said.
On the hydroelectric dam, Fadillah said it was Sarawak's first such dam, developed in 1985 in collaboration with a Japanese company, and was capable of generating 108 megawatts of power through four turbines.
"After 40 years of operation, the dam has had its lifespan extended by another 30 years, proving that the project is truly sustainable.
"Besides supplying green energy, the dam has also made significant contributions to Sarawak's economic development, particularly in the energy sector," he said.
Fadillah said a collaboration between Sarawak Energy Bhd (SEB) and a Chinese firm was also developing a floating solar project at the same location.
"The floating solar project is capable of generating around 50 megawatts of energy, further strengthening the use of renewable energy in the state.
"This will expand Sarawak's energy mix, which currently still relies between 60 and 70 per cent on hydro sources, to include solar and gas," he said.
Fadillah added that a floating hybrid solar initiative was also being implemented by Tenaga Nasional Bhd in Peninsular Malaysia, at Tasik Kenyir, with the capacity to generate 500 megawatts of power.
"The floating hybrid solar at Batang Ai generates 50MW of energy, and new projects are currently being implemented at the Bakun and Murum hydroelectric dams which, when operational, will together be capable of generating a total of 1,500MW of energy," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ramanan committed to strengthening school infrastructure, channels over RM100,000 since 2023
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Sungai Buloh member of parliament Datuk Seri R. Ramanan is committed to strengthening infrastructure and improving the quality of education at SMK Seksyen 8, Kota Damansara, through allocations amounting to more than RM100,000 since 2023.
The human resources minister said efforts to enhance educational facilities at the school will remain a key focus to ensure students enjoy a more conducive and quality learning environment.
"School infrastructure development and support for students are important investments for the nation's future. We will continue to ensure schools such as SMK Seksyen 8 Kota Damansara receive due attention so that the talents and potential of our children can be further developed," he said in a statement today.
Ramanan said, through the Sungai Buloh parliamentary service centre, various contributions and initiatives to improve school facilities have been implemented in stages, including the installation of air-conditioning systems in the computer laboratory, library, teachers' room, counselling and guidance unit room, and examination room in 2023, amounting to RM41,700.
In 2024, he said, RM17,034 was allocated for the purchase of air coolers and a public address system for the school hall, as well as RM10,000 for the Rakyat Tuition Programme organised by Yayasan Bank Rakyat.
Ramanan said RM21,800 was allocated for various school programmes, including the annual sports championship.
Overall, he said the total allocation and contributions channelled specifically to SMK Seksyen 8 Kota Damansara to date amounted to more than RM100,000.
"These ongoing initiatives reflect our continuous commitment to strengthening educational infrastructure and ensuring the welfare of students and school staff is well taken care of towards more holistic excellence," he said.
Ramanan also announced an allocation under Projek Mesra Rakyat to renovate the school surau following requests from the school community for a more comfortable and conducive environment for students.
He also said he had contacted Education Minister Fadhlina Sidek and Deputy Education Minister Wong Kah Woh to propose the construction of a new school field.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Padi farmers seek concrete action on diesel subsidies
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Padi farmers are urging the government to grant them access to subsidised diesel, warning that rising fuel costs are placing growing pressure on the sector.
The Malaysian Padi Farmers Association (Pesawah) said about 200,000 farmers nationwide are struggling with higher operating costs, particularly as they do not benefit from diesel subsidies available to other industries.
"We are not like fishermen or vegetable farmers who receive fleet card facilities," said its chairman Abdul Rashid Yob.
"Padi cultivation also relies heavily on diesel-powered machinery such as tractors, harvesters and water pumps. But we are excluded from subsidised fuel access," he said.
The appeal comes as global energy pressures continue to drive up fuel prices, increasing costs for farmers already facing tight margins.
In response, political secretary to the finance minister
Muhammad Kamil Abdul Munim
said the government is considering expanding the targeted diesel subsidy scheme, including the use of fleet cards for padi machinery operators.
The move is part of broader efforts to ease the burden on farmers affected by rising agricultural input costs linked to higher global fuel prices.
Calls for additional support
have also come from political leaders, including Kedah Umno chairman Datuk Seri Mahdzir Khalid, who has urged the government to introduce a more comprehensive mechanism to assist farmers.
Despite existing support measures, farmers say the assistance remains insufficient.
Abdul Rashid said subsidies for ploughing and harvesting — recently raised to RM160 and RM50 per hectare respectively — fall short of actual costs, which can reach RM450 and more than RM600 per hectare.
"Most farmers are forced to purchase diesel at market prices, which significantly increases machinery service costs," he said.
He added that delays in incentive payments, sometimes made only after several planting seasons, further strain farmers who must front the costs themselves.
At the same time, farmers are grappling with controlled prices for padi and rice, which have not kept pace with rising production costs.
"All costs have increased, including logistics and raw materials, but padi prices remain around RM1,500 per tonne," he said.
"If farmers cannot sustain operations, it will affect the entire supply chain, including millers, and ultimately impact national food security."
Pesawah said it has repeatedly raised the issue with the government but has yet to see concrete action on the proposed fleet card system.
The group is now urging authorities to review existing mechanisms and introduce more effective measures to address the ongoing energy crisis.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Article:

Iran's campaign had impacted output at Saudi's Manifa field, where production was now restored, the ministry said, and at the Khurais oil field, where work was underway to restore full production capacity.
On Friday, Riyadh confirmed the weeks-long Iranian attacks had impacted oil production capacity and killed one person.
Three people in total have been killed in Saudi Arabia since the start of the war.
An energy ministry official told the SPA news agency that one of the pumping stations on a vital east-west pipeline was hit in the attacks.
Saudi Arabia's Petroline proved an economic lifeline during the war, with the 750-mile network of pipes connecting the Red Sea to the west with the Gulf in the east as Iran effectively closed the waterway's critical Strait of Hormuz chokepoint.
Facilities in Riyadh, the Eastern Province and the industrial city of Yanbu as well as infrastructure for oil and gas production, transport and refining, and petrochemical plants and power facilities were targeted in the Iranian attacks on Saudi Arabia, the official said.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bukit Berapit ICQS to be upgraded after feasibility study - Saifuddin
Article:
GERIK: The Bukit Berapit Immigration, Customs, Quarantine and Security (ICQS) Complex here has been identified for a potential upgrade, with a RM2 million feasibility study underway to assess the suitability of the land for a new facility.
Home Minister Datuk Seri Saifuddin Nasution Ismail said the study will determine the viability of constructing a new ICQS complex at the site, as part of broader efforts to modernise border infrastructure.
He said the upgrade is aimed at improving efficiency, traveller comfort and safety at the crossing point, which features a unique arrangement where the Malaysian and Thai checkpoints are located less than one kilometre apart.
"I came here personally because Pengkalan Hulu is unique. The distance between our checkpoint and the Thai checkpoint is relatively short, at less than one kilometre.
"I observed earlier that travellers had to carry their luggage across, which raises certain safety concerns.
"Through discussions with the Perak menteri besar, several potential sites for a new facility have been proposed. At this stage, we are conducting a feasibility study.
"A sum of RM2 million has been allocated for this preliminary study. Ultimately, the aim is to improve comfort for travellers while ensuring that security is not compromised," he told reporters after his working visit to the Bukit Berapit Immigration, Customs, Quarantine and Security (ICQS) Complex in Pengkalan Hulu today.
Also present were Bukit Aman Internal Security and Public Order Department acting director Datuk Fisol Salleh, Malaysian Border Control and Protection Agency (MCBA) director-general Datuk Seri Mohd Shuhaily Mohd Zain, and Perak police chief Datuk Noor Hisam Nordin.
He said Bukit Berapit recorded about 180,000 traveller movements in the first three months of the year, compared with 735,000 at Padang Besar over the same period.
While acknowledging its strategic role as a northern entry point, he said enforcement data showed comparatively lower levels of smuggling and cross-border crime at Bukit Berapit compared with hotspots such as Padang Besar, Bukit Kayu Hitam, and several crossings in Kelantan.
He said the complex currently operates under the existing multi-agency framework and has yet to be placed under the administration of the Malaysian Border Control and Protection Agency (MCBA).
He said the agency is being rolled out in phases nationwide, with priority given to major entry points.
"At present, MCBA is still in its early stages. It is strengthening its manpower, systems, training and infrastructure," he said.
Saifuddin said 22 out of 122 entry points have so far been brought under MCBA in the first phase, focusing on key gateways such as KLIA, Johor checkpoints, and other major land, sea and air entry points.
He added that Bukit Berapit ICQS will be included in due course, based on operational readiness and national rollout priorities.
"Out of every 100 travellers entering Malaysia, about 75 use major entry points such as KLIA and Johor, while 25 per cent use other crossings," he said.
Saifuddin added that the phased implementation is necessary to ensure a smooth transition towards a single-agency border management system.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Woman loses RM307k in 'lucky draw' scam
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: An elderly woman was allegedly duped by two men into withdrawing RM307,000 from three banks in Rembau and Senawang.
At a press conference alongside MCA Bekok assemblyman Tan Chong, the 78-year-old victim, known as Gan, said the incident occurred last Friday, April 6, at 8am while she was walking to a nearby market.
She said a man approached her, claiming to recognise her as a former fruit seller.
"He asked if I recognised him and said he had previously bought fruit from me," she said.
Gan, who sold fruit and soy drinks for over 40 years before stopping six years ago, said the man later told her she had won RM200,000 in a bank lucky draw and asked her to return home to collect documents to claim the prize.
On the way, she met a neighbour and invited them along, but the man dismissed the neighbour and accompanied her to retrieve the documents.
She later got into the man's car in a dazed state.
Gan said she could not recall what happened next, with events later pieced together through closed-circuit television (CCTV) footage.
She recalled that two men were in the car — the Chinese man and a dark-skinned driver — both of whom smoked continuously.
She said the men claimed to be aged 23 and 37. The driver was also heard speaking in Malay on the phone, saying arrangements had been made with a bank manager to collect the "prize".
Gan's eldest son, Lim, said he arrived in Johor Bahru from Singapore at 10am that day. After returning to Labis, he tried calling his mother but could not reach her.
"At 1.30pm, she answered and said she was having tea with a friend, which we later realised was what the scammers told her to say. At 3pm, she called again saying she had been scammed," he said.
Gan later withdrew large sums from three banks, including RM210,000, RM30,000 and RM60,000 in cash, after transferring fixed deposits into savings accounts.
An additional RM7,000 in cash in her handbag was also taken.
Her daughter said bank staff became suspicious when one of the men kept whispering to Gan, who appeared unusually agitated.
The family has lodged a police report.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Masked men rob Kelantan Welfare Department office, flee with vehicles
Article:
TANAH MERAH: The Kelantan Welfare Department branch office here was robbed this morning by a group of masked men armed with knives, who fled with two four-wheel-drive vehicles.
Its director Che Samsuzuki Che Noh, when contacted, confirmed the incident, saying a security guard on duty at the premises was tied up, with both his hands and legs bound.
He said the incident happened about 4.30am and a police report was lodged by the department.
"We will leave it to the police to carry out their investigation into the case.
"We will also carry out our internal investigation," he added.
Meanwhile, a source said the suspects also took the security guard's wallet containing RM300 in cash.
"The robbers also took the keys to the security guard's motorcycle," the source said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: TA Securities expects profit rebound at PGF Capital
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: PGF Capital Bhd's latest quarterly core profit is projected to range between RM2 million and RM4.5 million against a core loss of RM1.9 million in the same period last year, TA Securities said.
The company is scheduled to announce its fourth-quarter financial year 2026 (FY26) results this month.
TA Securites said this is expected to ease seasonally from the previous quarter's core profit of RM7.8 million, as PGF Capital's shipments to Australia typically slow during the Christmas and New Year holiday period.
"Generally, we expect FY26 earnings to grow 40 per cent year-on-year on the back of robust demand for insulation products.
"As such, the on-going construction of a new plant in Kulim East is timely to increase the group's annual manufacturing capacity to 60,000 tonnes from 25,000 tonnes to fuel future earnings growth, in our opinion. This new plant is expected to be completed by mid-26," it said.
TA Securities said the US-Israel-Iran war is expected to indirectly raise packaging costs due to higher crude oil prices, but the overall earnings impact is considered minimal at below five per cent.
The firm said that PGF Capital's shipments were unaffected in the first quarter of 2026.
It added that financial year 2027 (FY27) first-quarter earnings growth should remain unchanged, supported by the company's typical three-month inventory buffer maintained in its Australian warehouses.
"However, we believe the hike in sea freight cost would likely dent profit margins from second quarter of FY27 onwards.
"As such, we revise the raw material cost, which is inclusive of transportation cost, higher by 4.9 per cent and 3.9 per cent for FY27 and FY28 respectively, assuming the hikes in sea freight expense and packaging cost will be felt from Q2 FY27," it said.
TA Securities said it remains cautious about a potential increase in electricity tariffs, which account for about one-third of production costs.
It added that PGF Capital currently bears the full cost of electricity without subsidies as a high-voltage industrial user.
The firm cut its FY27-FY28 earnings forecasts by 1.6 to 3.3 per cent, taking into account higher shipping and packaging costs.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: War in the Middle East: US-Iran negotiations fail as conflict intensifies across regions
Article:
PARIS: The latest developments in the Middle East war:
- KEY SAUDI PIPELINE BACK -
Saudi Arabia's energy ministry said that its key east-west oil pipeline and other facilities had been restored following attacks by Iran on infrastructure across the Gulf.
Citing an energy ministry statement, the official Saudi Press Agency reported that the attacks had led to a "loss of approximately 700,000 barrels per day of pumping capacity through the east-west pipeline" and work was under way to restore full production capacity at the kingdom's Khurais oil field.
- Teheran DOWNPLAYS NO-DEAL FINISH -
Iran's foreign ministry said no one had held any expectation that talks with the United States could have reached an agreement within one session after the negotiations in Islamabad stalled.
"Naturally, from the beginning we should not have expected to reach an agreement in a single session. No one had such an expectation," ministry spokesperson Esmaeil Baqaei said, according to state broadcaster IRIB.
- TALKS' END 'DISAPPOINTING' AUSTRALIA -
"The priority now must be to continue the ceasefire and return to negotiations," Australian Foreign Minister Penny Wong said in a statement, adding it was "disappointing that the Islamabad talks between the United States and Iran have ended without agreement."
- PAKISTAN URGES US, IRAN -
Pakistan's foreign minister insisted that Washington and Teheran must uphold a ceasefire agreement, after marathon talks between the two sides to end the war in the Middle East ended without a deal.
"It is imperative that the parties continue to uphold their commitment to ceasefire," said Ishaq Dar, whose government hosted the talks and acted as a mediator.
- VANCE LEAVES WITHOUT DEAL -
US Vice President JD Vance departed Pakistan aboard Air Force Two soon after saying that talks with Iran failed to reach an agreement after he put forward a "final and best offer."
"The simple fact is that we need to see an affirmative commitment that they will not seek a nuclear weapon and will not seek the tools that would enable them to quickly achieve a nuclear weapon," he told reporters in Islamabad, on the main sticking point for talks moving ahead.
- IRAN CALLS US 'UNREASONABLE' -
"The Iranian delegation negotiated continuously and intensively for 21 hours in order to protect the national interests of the Iranian people; despite various initiatives from the Iranian delegation, the unreasonable demands of the American side prevented the progress of the negotiations. Thus the negotiations ended," Iranian state broadcaster IRIB said.
- IRAN SAYS TALKS HINGED ON AVOIDING 'EXCESSIVE DEMANDS' -
The success of peace talks between the United States and Iran depended on Washington avoiding "excessive" and "unlawful" demands, the Iranian foreign ministry's spokesman said early on Sunday after marathon talks in Islamabad.
"The success of this diplomatic process depends on the seriousness and good faith of the opposing side, refraining from excessive demands and unlawful requests, and the acceptance of Iran's legitimate rights and interests," Esmaeil Baqaei wrote on X.
- IRAN AND US SPAR OVER STRAIT -
Iran denied Washington's claims that two US Navy warships crossed the Strait of Hormuz to clear the strategic waterway of mines, with Teheran warning that military ships attempting the passage "will be dealt with severely."
US Central Command's claim that mine detection operations were already underway was echoed by President Donald Trump, who told reporters Saturday "we have mine sweepers out there. We're sweeping the strait."
A fifth of the world's crude oil passes through the Strait of Hormuz.
- TRUMP SAYS IRAN DEAL 'MAKES NO DIFFERENCE' -
Trump told reporters Saturday it "makes no difference" if a peace deal comes out of the trilateral US-Iran talks in Pakistan, insisting the United States has already won the war.
The 79-year-old Republican's comments came as Vice President JD Vance was leading the US delegation in Islamabad, with discussions heading into a second day.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Corporate mafia probe: Two-pronged investigation under Amla and criminal laws
Article:
GERIK: Police are investigating allegations linked to a so-called "corporate mafia" case under anti-money laundering laws (Amla) and criminal provisions in a two-pronged probe.
Home Minister Datuk Seri Saifuddin Nasution Ismail said the investigation is ongoing, with police pursuing all leads, including recording statements from those believed to be involved, both in the country and abroad.
"First, is the police investigating this matter? The answer is yes. The police are conducting the investigation in a two-pronged approach.
"There are elements under Amla, and there are criminal elements as well. So we are doing both simultaneously, and the process is ongoing," he said.
He said this after a working visit to the Bukit Berapit Immigration, Customs, Quarantine and Security Complex in Pengkalan Hulu.
Also present were Bukit Aman Internal Security and Public Order Department acting director Datuk Fisol Salleh, Malaysian Border Control and Protection Agency director-general Datuk Seri Mohd Shuhaily Mohd Zain, and Perak police chief Datuk Noor Hisam Nordin.
He said this when asked whether police had traced businessman Victor Chin and if Interpol assistance could be sought for his extradition to the country.
Saifuddin said those required to assist the investigation would be called up if they are in the country, while those overseas may still be required to give statements through appropriate legal channels.
"The most important thing is that police have already started the
investigation
and progress is going well. I am being briefed from time to time by investigating officers," he said.
Chin is believed to have left the country a few days after being released by police in December last year.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hungarians vote in closely watched election with Orban's rule on line
Article:
BUDAPEST: Hungarians began voting on Sunday in closely watched parliamentary elections that could end nationalist Prime Minister Viktor Orban's 16-year stint in power as a self-described "thorn" in the EU's side.
US President Donald Trump has thrown his weight behind the EU's current longest-serving leader, who maintains close ties to Russia. The vote is closely followed abroad, particularly across the rest of Europe.
Opinion polls suggest the Tisza party of pro-European conservative Peter Magyar, who has promised a "system change", is running well ahead of Orban's.
Both camps have alleged foreign interference during the campaign in the central European country of 9.5 million people. US Vice President JD Vance visited Budapest earlier this week to rally with Orban.
Orban, 62, who is seeking a fifth straight term, has transformed his country into a model of "illiberal democracy."
Like Trump, he casts migration and "woke" values as a threat to Western "civilisation."
Former government insider Magyar, 45, burst onto the scene just two years ago, amassing support against a backdrop of economic stagnation, despite an electoral system skewed in favour of Orban's Fidesz party.
"Now is our last chance to choose finally east or west. Do we want to be a normal democracy or turn back east with no point of return?" first-time voter David Banhegyi, 18, told AFP after casting his ballot for Tisza in a leafy part of Budapest as a steady trickle of voters arrived.
Polls opened at 6am (0400 GMT), according to AFP journalists. They will close at 7pm.
Orban has increasingly locked horns with Brussels, which accuses him of quashing dissent and eroding the rule of law and has frozen billions of euros in EU funding.
During his visit, Vance attacked the alleged interference in Hungary of Brussels "bureaucrats", while Trump has promised to bring US "economic might" to Hungary if Orban's party secures victory.
While criss-crossing the country since February, Magyar has called on Hungarians to "take our homeland back" to secure its place in the EU, and promised to crack down on corruption and offer better services.
Hungary is seen as the most corrupt in the EU, together with Bulgaria, according to the Transparency International watchdog.
[[nid:1416016]]
"I have one main problem with this government, what it did, the robbing," said Edit Csillaghegyi, a 58-year-old shop worker, who told AFP that she voted for Magyar's Tisza.
But Maria Toth, a 31-year-old stay-at-home mother of two, told AFP that "it is so important for us that Viktor Orban stays in power,"
"I feel Hungary is under siege from so many directions and big powers like Brussels are trying to dictate how we live.
"If he loses, I worry for my children's future," she added after casting her ballot.
Orban has focused on making Ukraine the centre topic of his campaign, portraying the neighbouring country, which is fighting off a Russian invasion, as "hostile" to Hungary.
Ahead of the campaign, he has also vowed to continue his crackdown against "fake civil society organisations, bought journalists, judges (and) politicians."
"If Fidesz wins now, that will clearly mean... a shift towards authoritarianism," Andrea Szabo, a senior research fellow at ELTE University's Centre for Social Sciences, told AFP.
"This is the last moment in which this process can be halted, and the pendulum can swing back in a democratic direction," she added.
Analysts expect a record turnout of around 75 per cent, exceeding the previous record of just over 70 per cent.
The first preliminary results are expected soon after polls close, but if the race is tight the winner might not be declared until ballot counting is completely finished next Saturday, according to the National Election Office.
While the opposition fears Orban may not recognise the election results, the nationalist leader has accused the opposition of "an organised attempt to use chaos" and "colluding" with foreign intelligence.
In the lead-up to the vote, a flurry of accusations, counter-accusations and leaks surfaced, including recorded phone conversations that caused EU-wide alarm about Orban's and his foreign minister's close relations with Moscow.
Reports have claimed an ongoing covert Russian disinformation campaign to help Orban stay in power, while a documentary has alleged his ruling coalition is engaging in massive vote-buying efforts in rural districts.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Artemis crew urges unity on Earth after historic lunar flyby
Article:
HOUSTON: Artemis II astronauts expressed awe on Saturday over their record-setting lunar flyby mission, urging unity on Earth after witnessing the planet's isolation like a "lifeboat" in space.
The crew spoke at a news conference as NASA took a victory lap following the success of the mission.
The astronauts did the first slingshot around the Moon in more than 50 years and traveled deeper into space than any humans before – culminating in a smooth splashdown Friday off the coast of California.
Flanked on stage by mission commander Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover and Jeremy Hansen, astronaut Christina Koch called for people on Earth to embrace their shared humanity.
"What struck me wasn't necessarily just Earth, it was all the blackness around it. Earth was just this lifeboat hanging undisturbingly in the universe," said Koch.
"There's one new thing I know and that is: Planet Earth – you are a crew."
Hansen called on fans of the Artemis mission to see themselves in the crew of four, who marked a series of milestones: Glover was the first person of color to fly around the Moon, Koch was the first woman, and Canadian Hansen the first non-American.
"I would suggest to you that when you look up here, you're not looking at us. We are a mirror reflecting you," Hansen said.
"And if you like what you see, then just look a little deeper. This is you."
While hurtling through deep space and zipping around the Moon, the Artemis astronauts took thousands of photographs, amassing a stunning portfolio of images.
They also witnessed a solar eclipse along with extraordinary meteorite strikes on the lunar surface.
"Artemis II will always be remembered. It was the moment we all saw the moon again, where childhood dreams became missions. You helped the world start believing again, and this is something no one's ever going to forget," NASA administrator Jared Isaacman said.
Artemis II was the inaugural crewed mission of NASA's programme aiming to install a sustained presence on the Moon, including the eventual construction of a base that could be used for further exploration including to Mars.
NASA is hoping it can put boots on the lunar surface as soon as 2028.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ex-Kelantan footballer charged with kidnapping gold shop owner
Article:
PASIR MAS: A former Kelantan footballer and his accomplice were charged at the Sessions Court today with kidnapping a gold shop owner in May last year.
Che Hisammudin Hassan, 51, a former Kelantan football striker, and Saufi Ariffin, 24, both pleaded not guilty after the charge was read before Judge Zulkpli Abdullah.
They were accused of forcibly abducting Marzuki Mustapha with intent to cause him to be secretly and wrongfully confined at 7.20am along a roadside in Kampung Palekbang, Tumpat, on May 20 last year.
They were charged under Section 365 of the Penal Code read together with Section 34 of the same code, which, upon conviction, carries a jail term of up to seven years and a fine.
The prosecution was conducted by deputy public prosecutor Siti Aiysha Na'ilah Harizan.
Che Hisammudin was represented by lawyer Heather Marie Anthony, while Saufi was unrepresented.
The prosecution proposed bail of RM20,000 for each accused, but the court set bail at RM12,000 each and fixed May 18 for the next mention.
Media previously reported that Kelantan police were tracking four men believed to be involved in the
kidnapping
of a 44-year-old gold shop owner in Palekbang.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Saifuddin: Border petrol stations with unusual sales under police watch
Article:
GERIK: Petrol stations along Malaysia's borders recording unusually high fuel sales volumes will be placed under closer police scrutiny as part of a nationwide crackdown on smuggling, Home Minister Datuk Seri Saifuddin Nasution Ismail said.
He said enforcement agencies would prioritise stations with abnormal petrol and diesel sales using data shared by the Finance Ministry (MOF) and the Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry (KPDN).
"We will work smarter by engaging KPDN and MOF to obtain information on sales volumes at petrol stations near the borders.
"If the records show unusually high figures, these locations will become our main focus, as we cannot monitor everything at once," he told reporters after a working visit to the Bukit Berapit Immigration, Customs, Quarantine and Security (ICQS) Complex in Pengkalan Hulu today.
Also present were Bukit Aman Internal Security and Public Order Department acting director Datuk Fisol Salleh, Malaysian Border Control and Protection Agency director-general Datuk Seri Mohd Shuhaily Mohd Zain, and Perak police chief Datuk Noor Hisam Nordin.
Saifuddin said KPDN regulates about 4,000 petrol stations nationwide, while MOF oversees fuel subsidies, with both agencies holding relevant data.
"Once the data is shared, police will be deployed to focus on stations with unreasonable sales figures. This will greatly facilitate our operations," he said, adding that about 170 petrol stations are located along Malaysia's borders.
He said the move follows a Cabinet directive amid the ongoing Middle East crisis and global energy concerns, which have heightened the risk of fuel smuggling due to price disparities.
Saifuddin said the police have been tasked with strengthening monitoring at border petrol stations while maintaining their core responsibilities, including investigations, intelligence and public order.
"Our personnel strength is about 120,000, and our primary duties must not be compromised. However, we must also address smuggling activities that could harm the economy," he said.
On jurisdiction, he said offences involving controlled goods fall under KPDN, while smuggling and theft cases come under the Home Ministry.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia faces RM3.3bil kidney disease burden each year
Article:
SHAH ALAM: Malaysia is now spending RM3.3 billion annually on end-stage kidney disease treatment, a sharp rise from RM572 million in 2010, highlighting the growing burden of chronic illness on the country's healthcare system.
Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad said chronic kidney disease (CKD) has become one of the most pressing health challenges facing Malaysians today.
More than five million people in the country are estimated to be living with the condition, but only about five per cent are aware of it.
The prevalence of CKD has also risen significantly, from nine per cent in 2011 to 15.5 per cent in 2025.
"Every day, 28 Malaysians are diagnosed with kidney failure and must begin dialysis treatment," he said at the national-level World Kidney Day 2026 event in Shah Alam.
He warned that the disease is not only affecting quality of life, but also placing a heavy financial strain on the country.
"If we fail to act decisively now, more than 106,000 Malaysians will require dialysis treatment by 2040," he said.
Dzulkefly said most kidney failure cases in Malaysia are linked to complications from diabetes.
In response, the government has intensified efforts to curb sugar consumption, including raising the excise duty on sugary drinks to 90 sen per litre from January 2025.
The tax generated RM54.9 million in revenue last year, with RM21 million channelled back to the health ministry.
The funds are being used to finance Sodium-Glucose Transport Protein 2 (SGLT2) inhibitors, a class of medication shown to help treat diabetes and reduce the risk of kidney disease complications.
The ministry is also promoting a "peritoneal dialysis first" policy, encouraging eligible patients to undergo dialysis treatment at home.
The approach is aimed at easing congestion at treatment centres, lowering healthcare costs, and improving patients' quality of life.
In 2025, the ministry allocated RM40 million for peritoneal dialysis, with uptake rising to 42 per cent of patients in public health facilities, compared with 36.6 per cent in 2020.
A total of 3,161 patients have benefited directly from the treatment.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cambodia urges Thailand to resume talks on disputed border
Article:
PHNOM PENH: Cambodia has urged Thailand to resume talks as soon as possible on their disputed border, a long-standing disagreement that led to deadly clashes last year.
Cambodian Prime Minister Hun Manet said on Saturday that he "strongly hopes" the two neighbours will be able to work together "quickly and with sincerity."
"This will be foundation for long-lasting peace that would allow our people living along shared border to live peacefully. Cambodia is fully ready," he said in a statement posted on social media.
Thailand's Foreign Minister Sihasak Phuangketkeow said earlier Saturday that his country was not ready to resume talks, even though a new government had just been formed.
When asked about Cambodia's renewed call for negotiations, he said it had the "right" to do so, but that Thailand had its "own procedures" to follow.
The two Southeast Asian countries have been at odds for decades over the demarcation of their 800-kilometre (500-mile) border, a legacy of the French colonial era.
The dispute erupted into several rounds of clashes last year, killing dozens of people and displacing more than a million in July and December.
The countries signed a ceasefire agreement in late December that allows for border talks.
But tensions on the frontier remain, with both sides trading accusations of truce violations.
Cambodia claims Thai forces captured several areas in border provinces – contrary to their agreement – and has demanded their withdrawal.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Khaled questions if Malaysians ready for 'uncertain future' following peace talk breakdown
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Datuk Seri Mohamed Khaled Nordin has called for a serious reflection on national resilience following the reported failure of negotiations between Iran and the United States in Islamabad.
In a statement on his official Facebook page, the defence minister noted that the breakdown in talks between the two nations signals a period of heightened global instability.
"It appears that negotiations between Iran and the US in Islamabad have failed to reach any agreement. The world is set to face an uncertain future," he said.
Khaled raised questions regarding the preparedness of Malaysians to navigate the ripples of such geopolitical tensions.
"Are we, as Malaysians, ready for all possibilities? Are we a strong and resilient nation?"
His remarks come as global observers keep a close watch on Middle Eastern diplomatic efforts, where the lack of a resolution is expected to have far-reaching impacts on international security and economic stability.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Negotiation snag? US demanded Iran drop all nuclear programmes, including medical use
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The United States had demanded during peace negotiations with Iran in Islamabad that Teheran drop its nuclear programme entirely.
This was not just limited to any weapons programme, but also to the use of nuclear technology for civilian and medical purposes.
Al Jazeera reported that the proposed 10-point framework Iran had come up with stopped short of a complete abandonment of its nuclear ambitions.
However, the US demands included that Iran all nuclear programmes, civilian or otherwise.
The media organisation's representative in Islamabad reported that Iran will likely have to respond publicly to the US proposal and make clear what is actually on the table.
"The Iranians have been saying throughout this conflict that, for the last several years, there has been a decree by the supreme leader that they will not develop a nuclear weapon.
"This is something they came very close to agreeing to in Geneva and in Oman, and then they were attacked."
However, Al Jazeera reported that Iranian officials feel the US "continues to shift the goalposts", demanding that all nuclear programmes be stopped.
"There is a sea of mistrust that they are trying to build bridges over, and statements like this and leaving the negotiations with an ultimatum are not going to help bridge those divides.
"It appears the Iranians will have to come up with a response, and will probably have to make public what this deal is that the US has left on the table. So far, there is no clarity on what it contains."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Diesel hike squeezes supply chain as tofu, soya products see price increase in KL
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 12 — Even the humble tofu, often seen as a cheaper alternative to meat, is beginning to feel the pinch following weeks of sharp diesel price hikes.
In some parts of the capital, prices of tofu and soya products have climbed by nearly 10 per cent since April 7, forcing traders to pass on higher costs to consumers.
Kamol Cheng Thean, a vegetable and soy products trader at Pasar Harian Selayang, said prices of dry, fresh, fried and five-spiced tofu have increased by 10 sen per piece, while pressed tofu has gone up by 20 sen per piece.
Tofu prices have gone up by 10 to 20 cents per piece since April 7, following sharp diesel price hikes. — Unsplash pic
Soya bean drinks are also selling 50 sen higher per kilogram now, said the 55-year-old trader.
“Before this, one piece of tofu was about 50 sen but now I have to sell it for 60 sen.
“The supplier did not say if the prices will increase further but if the fuel prices go up, the tofu price will also go up,” Kamol told Malay Mail when met at his shop today.
Malaysia is a net importer of soybeans, primarily from the United States, used to produce tofu and soya bean drinks as well as animal feed for the poultry industry.
However, higher fertiliser costs due to reduced production, shipping disruptions at the Strait of Hormuz, and rising packaging costs have pushed soybean prices higher.
Locally, diesel prices in Peninsular Malaysia have surged from RM2.99 per litre in mid-February to a record RM6.72 per litre last week.
Cheaper greens but few takers
Despite recent fertiliser price increases and the absence of price controls, vegetables are still being sold at relatively affordable rates at Pasar Harian Selayang, one of the capital’s largest vegetable supply hubs.
Spinach (bayam) and water spinach (kangkung) are sold at around RM2.50 per kg, while cabbage and sawi (mustard greens) are priced at about RM3 and RM4 per kg respectively.
However, Pasar Harian Selayang Traders’ Association vice-chairman Sum Sing, 70, said prices of small cucumbers have risen from RM3 per kg last week to RM5 per kg recently, while large cucumbers are now selling at RM4 per kg.
Vegetable vendor Uma Pathy Marutapa said the rise in prices was linked to reduced cucumber harvests caused by recent dry spells and heatwaves, which may have led to premature flower dropping.
Uma added that some suppliers have hinted at potential price increases if diesel prices rise further.
Vegetable vendor Uma Pathy Marutapa is already bracing for a possible vegetable price hike if fuel prices continue to soar. — Picture by Raymond Manuel
However, she said she was more concerned about a sharp drop in customer footfall in the vegetable section, adding that vendors had better business when they were located beside the seafood section on the ground floor.
“I only make a profit of 50 sen per kg for each vegetable and the prices fluctuate all the time.
“But the real problem is fewer customers walk up to our section because they find it easier to buy vegetables from foreigners selling them by the roadside outside the market,” she said.
But the situation is not much better for those trading outside the market.
A Burmese vegetable seller, who only wanted to be known as Cho, said his stall survives mainly by supplying vegetables to restaurants and eateries, as fewer people now visit the market to buy groceries.
Baskets of fresh vegetables lay idle outside Cho’s shop, located opposite Pasar Harian Selayang, despite him selling tomatoes and water spinach (kangkung) for as low as RM1.50 per kg.
“Families prefer to buy from supermarkets like NSK Grocer rather than coming to wet markets.
“So even if I sell at a cheaper price right by the roadside, there are very few takers,” he said.

Title: Police may use extradition to bring back overseas suspects in ‘corporate mafia’ probe, says home minister
Article:
PENGKALAN HULU, April 12 — The police will utilise all legal instruments, including extradition, to assist in investigations into alleged corporate manipulation activities, says Home Minister Datuk Seri Saifuddin Nasution Ismail.
He said the ongoing investigation into the case was being conducted under the Penal Code and the Anti-Money Laundering Act (AMLA).
“If any individuals are overseas, and there is a need to record their statements to assist in completing the investigation, we will use all available processes to enable us to bring them back.
“The investigation is proceeding well. I am briefed from time to time by the police conducting the investigation. I have no reason to doubt the competence of the police,” he said.
Saifuddin was speaking at a press conference after conducting a visit and monitoring border control operations at the Malaysia-Thailand Immigration, Customs, Quarantine and Security (ICQS) Complex in Bukit Berapit here today.
On March 18, Inspector-General of Police Datuk Seri Mohd Khalid Ismail was reported to have said that businessman Victor Chin, allegedly involved in corporate manipulation activities, was abroad. — Bernama

Title: Diesel refuelling glitch in Segambut caused by empty tank, missing auto cut-off system, deputy minister says after viral claims
Article:
PUTRAJAYA, April 12 — A viral diesel refuelling incident at a petrol station in Jalan Segambut, Kuala Lumpur was caused by depleted fuel stock and the absence of an automatic volume detection system.
Deputy Minister of Domestic Trade and Cost of Living, Datuk Dr Fuziah Salleh, said this was based on findings from an investigation by the Ministry of Domestic Trade and Cost of Living (KPDN), following the circulation of a video on social media.
She said immediate investigations by a Kuala Lumpur KPDN enforcement team found that the diesel supply had run out while refuelling was still in progress.
“In the incident, the pump metre continued recording the amount charged even though the diesel supply had been depleted,” she said in a statement today.
Further investigations revealed that the station was not equipped with an automatic volume detection system or an automatic cut-off feature when fuel runs out, and instead relied on manual dipping.
Fuziah said checks confirmed that the actual amount of diesel dispensed was RM232.07.
“The station took prompt action by issuing a full refund to the affected customer on April 9, 2026, and the complainant agreed to the resolution,” she said.
She added that technical inspections by KPDN found all fuel pumps at the premises were still within the valid verification period.
“Tests using a 20-litre Liquid Volume Measure (SIC) showed readings within the permitted margin of error,” she said.
Further checks confirmed that the station’s licences under the Control of Supplies Act 1961 and the Petroleum Development Act 1974 remain valid.
“However, the station has been given a stern warning to fully comply with all regulations and to improve operational monitoring to prevent a recurrence,” she said.
Fuziah stressed that the incident was isolated and does not reflect the overall fuel supply system, which remains stable and under control.
The public has been advised not to speculate or spread unverified information.
“KPDN will continue to strengthen monitoring and enforcement to safeguard consumer interests,” she said. — Bernama

Title: New Sarawak cancer hospital aims to serve patients across Borneo; design complete, tender next
Article:
KUCHING, April 12 — The proposed Sarawak Cancer Centre is expected to commence construction in 2027 following the completion of its design phase, said Deputy Health Minister Datuk Hanifah Hajar Taib.
She said the project has already completed its design stage and is expected to proceed to the tender process before construction begins.
“We have been informed that the design has already been completed and the project will proceed to the tender process.
“Once the tender is approved, InsyaAllah the target is for construction to begin in 2027,” she told reporters when met after the launch of the biography ‘Laila Taib: Her Journey of Love & Compassion’ and the exhibition ‘The Tatars – Muslim in the Republic of Poland’ at Pustaka Negeri Sarawak here on Sunday.
Hanifah said the establishment of the specialised cancer hospital would strengthen ongoing cancer treatment and research efforts currently taking place at Sarawak General Hospital.
“At Sarawak General Hospital here in Kuching, they already have clinical research which is actually among the first in Malaysia trying to address cancer issues.
“They have achieved a very high level of progress, more than 90 per cent, and they welcome those who want to try treatment in the hope of full recovery,” she said.
She noted that although knowledge and research on cancer have progressed significantly, the disease remains complex due to the wide variety of cancer types that require different treatment approaches.
“Our understanding of cancer has deepened greatly, but there are many different types of cancer, so we still need exposure and research on types that have yet to be studied,” she said.
Hanifah added that doctors and researchers are continuously working to improve treatment methods and better understand how cancer affects patients.
“The aim is to understand how to handle different types of cancer, how it affects the patient and the body, and how treatment can continue to improve,” she said.
She said the proposed hospital is intended to address the growing demand for specialised cancer care in the region.
“If we look at the needs in Sarawak, the facilities are still not sufficient. So it is appropriate that we have a hospital specifically focused on cancer,” she said.
According to Hanifah, the facility is envisioned to serve not only Sarawak but also neighbouring regions across Borneo.
“The intention that has been shared by the Premier is that this hospital is for the interest of Borneo.
“It is not only for Sarawak but also for Sabah and even Kalimantan,” she said.
She added that the hospital could help reduce the need for patients from the region to travel far for specialised treatment.
“Sometimes it is not easy for people to obtain treatment if everything is concentrated in Kuala Lumpur. For those in the rural areas, access is limited,” she added. — The Borneo Post

Title: Police to focus on high-sales petrol stations near borders in new anti-smuggling move, says Saifuddin Nasution
Article:
PENGKALAN HULU, April 12 — The Home Ministry (KDN) will assess fuel sales volumes at petrol stations near the country’s borders to implement enforcement measures aimed at curbing fuel smuggling.
Home Minister Datuk Seri Saifuddin Nasution Ismail said the ministry will get all sales data from the Finance Ministry and the Ministry of Domestic Trade and Cost of Living (KPDN) to identify petrol stations near the country’s borders which recorded unreasonable sales.
“Once these sales records are shared with us, we will deploy police teams to zoom in directly on such petrol stations… it will facilitate our operations.
